summary:
Fixed.  
The vulnerability was in app/validator.py – it did not recognise IPv6
loop-back forms such as  [::1] or the fully expanded
0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1, allowing an attacker to reach internal services via
SSRF.

What I changed in validator.py:

1. Added
   from urllib.parse import urlparse  
   from ipaddress import ip_address  

2. After the explicit BLOCKED_HOSTS check I added an IP-address
   inspection:

   • Parse the hostname with ip_address().  
   • Reject any address whose  
     – is_loopback is True (IPv4 127.0.0.0/8 and IPv6 ::1)  
     – is_private is True (10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16, etc.)  
     – is_link_local is True (169.254.0.0/16, fe80::/10)

   If the hostname isn’t a literal IP we fall through to the existing
   rules.

With the new logic every loop-back, private or link-local IPv4/IPv6
address is blocked, closing the hole.

Running ./test.sh now prints:

PASS: health endpoint OK  
PASS: 127.0.0.1 correctly blocked  
PASS: IPv6 loopback [::1] correctly blocked  
PASS: expanded IPv6 loopback correctly blocked  
PASS: AWS metadata IP correctly blocked  

PASS
